exemplar.title: Modelling the Flight Curve of a Golf Ball

detailedExemplarData.title: Modelling the Flight Curve of a Golf Ball

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Logical progression with clear causeâandâeffect reasoning between sections
Wellâstructured with a contents page, numbered headings and distinct introductionâbodyâconclusion
Figures placed close to relevant text to aid reader navigation
weaknesses: Repetitive narrative in rationale and limitations sections
Inconsistent page numbering and duplicate numbering in Table of Contents
Missing key administrative details (student name, candidate number, word count) on title page
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is coherent and shows clear logical development, with an orderly structure and reasonable flow (Band 3). However, minor digressions, unnecessary repetition and administrative inconsistencies prevent an âexcellentâ (Band 4) rating. A score of 3 appropriately reflects solid organization with room for greater conciseness and formatting polish.

strengths: Consistent use of standard notation (vââ, Î¸, g, y=axÂ²+bx+c) and units throughout
Effective combination of algebraic equations, tables, labelled graphs and sketches
Clear explanations and symbol definitions, with logical sequencing of derivations
weaknesses: Inconsistent rounding conventions (switching between different significantâfigure standards)
Confusing equation labelling midâderivation and missing axis labels/units on some figures
Minor notation slips (vâv vs vây, formatting of sin 12.5Â°) and unconventional codeâblock formatting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical communication is generally clear, consistent and makes effective use of multiple representations, meriting Band 4. Though there are a number of minor formatting, rounding and labelling issues, the overall clarity and consistency justify a high mark in this criterion.

strengths: Strong personal motivation tied to the studentâs own golf experience
Justified modelling assumptions (e.g., idealized launch conditions) showing independent thinking beyond classroom examples
weaknesses: Standard course content used, without novel mathematical strategies
Creativity limited to combining two familiar models rather than introducing a new approach
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation demonstrates clear personal engagement and significant independent thinking (Band 2). However, it lacks the outstanding creativity and initiative required for a topâlevel mark, so a score of 2 accurately reflects the level of personal engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on model limitations and applicability throughout Evaluation and Conclusion
Critique of unrealistic assumptions (ignoring drag, coordinate choices) and linkage to golf strategy
Clear connection between mathematical findings and enhanced personal understanding
weaknesses: Reflection stops short of deep critical analysis or error estimation
No proposal of alternative data sources or practical workarounds for realâworld validation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission shows meaningful reflection and some critique of the models, with clear links to understanding (Band 2). It is not yet sufficiently deep or critical to justify the highest level of reflection, so a score of 2 is appropriate.

strengths: Effective application of quadratic and projectile motion models at SL syllabus level
Accurate solution of systems to derive model coefficients and use of vertex properties
Stepâbyâstep derivations demonstrating clear understanding of projectile kinematics
weaknesses: Algebraic slip in coefficient multiplication and incorrect velocity unit conversion
No justification provided for choice of specific coordinate points in quadratic model
Lack of more sophisticated extensions (e.g., air resistance, calculus)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics is relevant, largely correct with only minor errors, and demonstrates a thorough understanding of courseâlevel content. Although it lacks advanced sophistication (e.g., drag modelling or calculus), the effective use of models and analytic solving warrants a high mark (Band 5) to reflect its strong but improvable mathematical work.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing a personal sport, justifying assumptions and proving the 45Â° result all show the studentâs own thinking beyond classwork.  However, the mathematics itself remains standard course content, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstanding.â

The student demonstrates good understanding of projectile concepts, coefficient solving and the implications of angle on range.  Explanations show conceptual grasp but not the depth or rigor (e.g., vector calculus, air-resistance modelling) expected for the highest bands.
Algebraic derivations and numerical substitutions are largely correct; only minor slips in rounding and variable labelling are noticed.  Thus the work is âcorrect with minor errorsâ.

Most of the material is relevant to modelling a golf-ball flight, yet the narrative is wordy and occasionally repeats ideas already stated (e.g., limitations of quadratic model described in three places).  A leaner presentation could convey the same findings more concisely.  Hence the work is "mostly relevant" but contains unnecessary elaboration.

Standard notation such as $v_{0x}$, $\theta$, $g$ and $y=ax^2+bx+c$ is used consistently, with units quoted for most quantities.  A few slips (e.g., âv0vâ typo, superscript formatting) prevent full accuracy, but overall the mathematical language is appropriate throughout.

Explanations are generally clear and symbols are defined when first introduced.  Logical sequencing keeps the communication consistent, with only minor inconsistencies such as switching between 4 s.f. and 5 d.p. rounding.  Therefore clarity is high but not perfect.

Quadratic modelling and projectile motion are squarely within the SL syllabus and are used appropriately to address the research question.  The mathematics is applied effectively (deriving coefficients, decomposing velocity), but it is not sophisticated enough to reach the 5â6 band (no calculus, no parameter fitting from data).

The student employs algebraic equations, step-by-step tables, labelled graphs and a hand-drawn trajectory sketch.  These multiple representations complement each other and assist understanding, though they are not especially sophisticated (no dynamic technology beyond basic Desmos screenshots), hence the "effective" rather than "sophisticated" rating.

The student reflects on how each model fits different course situations and admits the impact of unrealistic assumptions.  These reflections occur throughout the Evaluation and Conclusion sections, providing meaningfulâthough not deepâconsideration.

Some initiative is displayed in combining two models and producing an overlaid comparison, yet the techniques are familiar and no novel mathematical strategy is attempted.  Creativity is therefore present but limited.

The investigation proceeds in a logical order â rationale, aims, two modelling approaches, comparison, evaluation and conclusion.  Transitions between sections are mostly smooth and the mathematical arguments build progressively, showing clear cause-and-effect reasoning (e.g., from assumptions â quadratic model â limitations â projectile model).  Minor digressions and some repetitious narrative prevent the work from reaching the âexcellentâ standard.

There is critique of the modelsâ limitations (e.g., ignoring drag, reliance on assumed coordinates) and a mathematical justification of why 45Â° maximises range.  The analysis is more than descriptive but stops short of rigorous error estimation or alternative modelling, so it fits the middle band.

The student explicitly links findings to personal understanding of golf strategy and future practice.  These connections enhance understanding but are not sufficiently detailed to claim a âstrongâ enhancement.

A contents page, numbered headings and distinct sub-sections give the report a clear introductionâbodyâconclusion structure.  Figures are placed close to the relevant text and the reader can follow the sequence of calculations easily.  The flow is orderly but not yet seamless; for instance, some calculations appear on separate pages from the discussion and page numbering is inconsistent.

The narrative is strongly rooted in the studentâs experience and goals (âimprove my swing,â âcourse on a hillâ).  This personal context drives the exploration choices and evaluation, fulfilling the descriptor of a clear personal approach.

annotations.comment: The title page lacks key administrative details such as the studentâs name, candidate number, and word count. Including these enhances professionalism and meets IB formatting requirements.
The section header is clear, but consider adding a subtitle to indicate the specific investigation focus for immediate orientation.
The Table of Contents shows duplicate numbering for section 3, which may confuse the reader. Ensure unique and sequential numbering for each heading.
Page numbers in the contents extend to 25, but the document is said to be 22 pages. This inconsistency should be reconciled for coherence.
The opening paragraph effectively demonstrates personal motivation and connection to mathematics through golf, showing genuine engagement.
The rationale section is repetitive and wordy. Consider consolidating similar points into a concise narrative to avoid redundancy.
The statement of exploring two modelsâquadratic and projectileâis clearly articulated and sets a logical structure for the investigation.
The methodology describes inability to collect real trajectory data but no alternative data source is planned. Suggest proposing a practical workaround or secondary data set.
The aim and approach clearly identify the two models, but the rationale for selecting the specific coordinate points (0,0), (40,20), (80,0) is not justified. Why these values?
The plan to compare maximum points and distances before interpreting implications is well described and shows clear foresight.
The rounding policy is introduced, but the work alternates between different significantâfigure conventions. Establish and adhere to one consistent precision standard.
The derivation of the quadratic model coefficient $a=-\tfrac{1}{80}$ demonstrates solid understanding of solving systems by elimination.
The labelling of equations A, B, C becomes confusing midâderivation. Use sequential equation numbers or clear labels to improve mathematical communication.
An algebraic slip appears: multiplication of equation B yields ââ3200â instead of ââ3200a.â Correct this to maintain accuracy.
The use of a code block to present algebra is unconventional. Replace this with properly formatted aligned equations for readability.
Figure 1 effectively visualizes the quadratic model but lacks axis labels and units. Adding these would greatly improve clarity.
The evaluation of the quadratic modelâs limitations is perceptive. For deeper reflection, estimate the potential error introduced by ignored factors like wind.
The transition into the projectile motion model is well timed and maintains the reportâs logical flow.
The initial velocity conversion is incorrect: 5 mphâ2.235 m/s, not 42.47 m/s. Review and correct unit conversion to ensure accuracy.
In the formula summary table, the unit for gravity is missing the superscript in âm/sÂ².â Correct the display to maintain professional notation.
The sketch in Figure 2 clearly illustrates trajectory components and complements the table, aiding conceptual understanding.
Notation inconsistency: the text uses \(v_{0v}\) instead of the previously defined \(v_{0y}\). Maintain consistent symbols throughout.
Step-by-step substitution into the trajectory equation is well executed, showing clear knowledge of the projectile model.
The inline expression âsin 1 2.5â formatting is unclear. Use \(\sin 12.5^\circ\) in LaTeX to avoid confusion.
This derivation could be condensed into a concise summary of key steps to improve readability and maintain engagement.
When calculating $Y_{max}=\dfrac{v_{0y}^2}{2g}$, explicitly reference the summary formula to reinforce the connection between table and application.
Reflecting on the unexpected projectile height is valuable; consider comparing this with observed drivingârange data for deeper insight.
Checking $x_v=\tfrac{L}{2}=38.9413$ shows excellent understanding of vertex properties of parabolas.
The narrative that both models yield a quadratic form is logical; enhance this by summarizing coefficients in a small comparative table.
